includedPullRequests.title: Fix temp target presets showing up in the main chart
includedPullRequests.body:
Fixes #1324

Saving a new Temp Target as a preset made it show up in the main chart even though it was never activated. The chart's fetch predicate freezes its date anchors when it is built and only re-anchors when the app comes to the foreground, so a fresh preset (date now, enabled false) matched the clause meant for scheduled temp targets. The same stale anchor also kept a cancelled temp target's full bar on the chart until the app was backgrounded.

Presets are now excluded from the scheduled clause, and the fetched results are filtered again with a fresh date before they reach the chart. Added tests covering the predicate and the visibility rules.

includedPullRequests.title: Make bolus-in-progress card in Treatments view more reliable
includedPullRequests.body:
Some manual boluses left a bolusing card stuck at 0% in the Treatments view that blocked further boluses and could not be dismissed until the app was restarted (#1308).

The card was gated on bolusStatus, which mirrors the pump's reported bolusState and can stay at inProgress after delivery has finished. Since bolusTrigger now retains that value, the card resurfaced once a new amount was entered, and the X could not clear it because there was no active bolus to cancel.

Gate the card on bolusProgress instead, the local dose reporter signal that clears reliably and that the Home view already uses, keeping the initiating state so the pre-delivery view still shows.

includedPullRequests.title: Fix duplicate Nightscout treatment uploads by serializing per-pipeline runs
includedPullRequests.body:
## Problem

Trio occasionally uploads the same treatment to Nightscout twice — most visibly Exercise entries for overrides enacted via Shortcuts or remote commands. Reproduced with an APNS remote command producing two documents with millisecond-identical `created_at` and consecutive Mongo `_id`s, i.e. two near-simultaneous POSTs:

- Enacting an override via App Intents / remote control triggers uploads twice: the Core Data save fires the upload pipeline, and `.willUpdateOverrideConfiguration` fires a direct, unthrottled upload. Both fetch the same rows still marked `isUploadedToNS == false` before either has marked them uploaded.
- Nightscout's treatment upsert is a non-atomic find-then-insert (no unique index), so concurrent identical POSTs both insert. Sequential re-posts are deduped server-side; only concurrent ones slip through. (API v3 has the same race, so switching API versions wouldn't help.)
- The 2s throttle didn't prevent this: it only spaced out task starts within one trigger path, never waited for the previous upload to finish, and with `latest: false` silently dropped triggers arriving inside its window. Carb edits had a third, entirely unthrottled path (`HistoryStateModel` calls `uploadCarbs()` directly).

## Solution

Replace the per-pipeline subject/throttle machinery with `NightscoutUploadSerializer`, an actor that coalesces and serializes upload runs per pipeline:

- An idle pipeline starts a run immediately; while a run is in flight, exactly one follow-up is queued and further requests coalesce into it.
- No two runs of a pipeline ever overlap: a follow-up re-fetches only after the previous run marked its rows uploaded, so it finds nothing left to send. Queued runs double as retry attempts after network failures.
- The upload routine is provided once at init; `request(_:)`/`run(_:)` take only the pipeline, so every entry point (Core Data triggers, App Intents, remote control, direct calls) is covered with no call-site changes.
- App Intents semantics are preserved: `uploadOverrides()`/`uploadTempTargets()` are awaitable, so `.didUpdateOverrideConfiguration` still posts only after the upload attempt finished.

## Benefits

- No more duplicate treatments, regardless of how many trigger paths fire for the same change.
- Uploads start immediately — the fixed 2-second throttle delay is gone.
- No dropped triggers (the old throttle discarded a second change arriving within 2 s; it stayed unuploaded until the next unrelated trigger).
- `uploadDeviceStatus()` no longer throws; its only caller was the deleted pipeline dispatch, and errors are logged in `performUpload(for:)`.

Unit tests cover the serializer: no overlap under concurrency, queued-run ordering, burst coalescing into a single follow-up, completion semantics, and pipeline independence.

includedPullRequests.title: [Part 5 of 6] Remove JSON encoding for pump events
includedPullRequests.body:
This commit removes the old:
* CoreData -> DTOs -> JSON string -> [PumpHistoryEvent]

pipeline and replaces it with a direct pipeline:
* CoreData -> [PumpHistoryEvent]

The result of the two pipelines should be identical, except for the
handling of the subtype tempType of temp basal events, which we
nullify if we get an unrecognized string vs crashing as the old
pipeline would. This change is safe as Trio only ever records
"absolute" types and the value is copied but never actually used by
the algorithm.

includedPullRequests.title: Fix Temp Target and Override delete confirmation auto-dismissing
includedPullRequests.body:
Fixes #1314

Deleting a Temp Target or Override preset by swiping was unreliable. The delete confirmation only appeared for a moment before auto-dismissing, so the preset often couldn't be deleted.

The confirmation dialog was attached to the ForEach inside the List and driven by two state variables set at the same time, which made it dismiss itself. Reworked it to match the History screen: a single optional drives the dialog via presenting:, attached once to the view. Applied to both Temp Targets and Overrides.

includedPullRequests.title: [Part 6 of 6] Remove OpenAPSSwift.swift and use native arguments
includedPullRequests.body:
This commit is the last part of our removal of the JSON conversion we
used to do when we ran the oref algorithm using Javascript. In this
commit, we remove the OpenAPSSwift file and just call the underlying
algorithm functions directly from OpenAPS.

One change in functionality is that we expose raw DeterminationError
messages instead of catching them and converting them to APSError
exceptions with a generic "No determination error" error
message. These exception messages will be user visible when they
happen.

includedPullRequests.title: Remove explicit allowsConstrainedNetworkAccess setting for Nightscout uploads
includedPullRequests.body:
## Summary

This PR removes the explicit `allowsConstrainedNetworkAccess` setting from all Nightscout upload requests.

This restores the default `URLRequest` behavior and lets iOS decide how constrained network access should be handled according to the user's system settings.

## Background

While investigating intermittent Nightscout upload failures, repeated `Constrained path prohibited` errors were observed in the logs. This PR follows the review discussion and removes the explicit flag instead of overriding the system default.

includedPullRequests.title: Core Data Performance Improvements, Fixes and Adjustments
includedPullRequests.body:
**Core Data Context Rework**
Eliminates long-lived shared `NSManagedObjectContext` instances across all storages and service classes which was the reason for inconsistencies in the synchronization of our background contexts and probably also the root cause for the crash that was solved differently with one of my previous PRs #1100. Each data operation now creates a fresh task context via `CoreDataStack.shared.newTaskContext()`, preventing cross-context data races and stale object access. Every background context now is only active for a special task and it has the latest data from the `PersistentStore`. This is done similarly in Apples Earthquakes Sample: https://github.com/TomaszLizer/Apple-Earthquakes/

Those changes need to be tested thoroughly. I am testing it on myself for about 2 months now without any problems. But in theory I could have accidentally introduced a wrong `NSManagedObjectContext` access in a class that I simply don't use. This should be deteced via our `ConcurrencyDebug` flag though which then makes the app crash. So be aware of that.

 **N+1 Query Elimination**
 Adds `relationshipKeyPathsForPrefetching` for bolus/tempBasal on all `PumpEventStored` fetches (`OpenAPS`, `APSManager`, `WatchManager`, `GarminManager`)and `forecastValues` on `OrefDetermination` fetches. Eliminates hundreds of individual `WHERE Z_PK = ?` round-trips per Loop that I could identify in the SQLDebug logs. When we were fetching a pump event with its bolus relationship for example the relationships don't get loaded automatically, but instead created as a fault. So when we were doing 1 query only to iterate over the items to get its bolus property after that this was unefficient as this would fire several faults. `relationshipKeyPathsForPrefetching` gets rid of this as the bolus property is now already in the context cache after the first query.

**TDD Aggregate Optimization**
Replaces full ZTDDStored row loads (up to 1,041 rows) in `calculateWeightedAverage` with a single SQL aggregate query `(SELECT total(), COUNT())` — reducing fetch time from ~6 ms to ~0.8 ms per call.

**Carb/FPU & Determination Prefetch**
 Adds `SELF IN` batch-prefetch hop for CarbEntryStored and OrefDetermination in the Home state model to prevent N+1 faults during `getNSManagedObject` materialization on the view context.

**NSFetchedResultsController migration**
New NSFetchedResultsControllerDelegate plus migration of HomeStateModel, TreatmentsStateModel, NightscoutManager, TidepoolManager, and HealthKitManager to FRCs bound to the viewContext, replacing the previous objectID/publisher logic.

**History-sourced change feed (CoreDataObserver.swift)**
The hand-rolled `changedObjectsOnManagedObjectContextDidSavePublisher` (based on `NSManagedObjectContextDidSave`) is replaced by `CoreDataStack.entityChangePublisher`, sourced from persistent history. This now also captures batch operations and cross-process changes.

**New dedup logic in `PumpHistoryStorage`**
The new dedup key combines `timestamp` AND `type` so that a bolus and temp basal event can coexist at the same time. Also add a unique constraint that ensures this behaviour.

includedPullRequests.title: Core Data Performance Improvements, Fixes and Adjustments
includedPullRequests.body:
**Core Data Context Rework**
Eliminates long-lived shared `NSManagedObjectContext` instances across all storages and service classes which was the reason for inconsistencies in the synchronization of our background contexts and probably also the root cause for the crash that was solved differently with one of my previous PRs #1100. Each data operation now creates a fresh task context via `CoreDataStack.shared.newTaskContext()`, preventing cross-context data races and stale object access. Every background context now is only active for a special task and it has the latest data from the `PersistentStore`. This is done similarly in Apples Earthquakes Sample: https://github.com/TomaszLizer/Apple-Earthquakes/

Those changes need to be tested thoroughly. I am testing it on myself for about 2 months now without any problems. But in theory I could have accidentally introduced a wrong `NSManagedObjectContext` access in a class that I simply don't use. This should be deteced via our `ConcurrencyDebug` flag though which then makes the app crash. So be aware of that.

 **N+1 Query Elimination**
 Adds `relationshipKeyPathsForPrefetching` for bolus/tempBasal on all `PumpEventStored` fetches (`OpenAPS`, `APSManager`, `WatchManager`, `GarminManager`)and `forecastValues` on `OrefDetermination` fetches. Eliminates hundreds of individual `WHERE Z_PK = ?` round-trips per Loop that I could identify in the SQLDebug logs. When we were fetching a pump event with its bolus relationship for example the relationships don't get loaded automatically, but instead created as a fault. So when we were doing 1 query only to iterate over the items to get its bolus property after that this was unefficient as this would fire several faults. `relationshipKeyPathsForPrefetching` gets rid of this as the bolus property is now already in the context cache after the first query.

**TDD Aggregate Optimization**
Replaces full ZTDDStored row loads (up to 1,041 rows) in `calculateWeightedAverage` with a single SQL aggregate query `(SELECT total(), COUNT())` — reducing fetch time from ~6 ms to ~0.8 ms per call.

**Carb/FPU & Determination Prefetch**
 Adds `SELF IN` batch-prefetch hop for CarbEntryStored and OrefDetermination in the Home state model to prevent N+1 faults during `getNSManagedObject` materialization on the view context.

**NSFetchedResultsController migration**
New NSFetchedResultsControllerDelegate plus migration of HomeStateModel, TreatmentsStateModel, NightscoutManager, TidepoolManager, and HealthKitManager to FRCs bound to the viewContext, replacing the previous objectID/publisher logic.

**History-sourced change feed (CoreDataObserver.swift)**
The hand-rolled `changedObjectsOnManagedObjectContextDidSavePublisher` (based on `NSManagedObjectContextDidSave`) is replaced by `CoreDataStack.entityChangePublisher`, sourced from persistent history. This now also captures batch operations and cross-process changes.

**New dedup logic in `PumpHistoryStorage`**
The new dedup key combines `timestamp` AND `type` so that a bolus and temp basal event can coexist at the same time. Also add a unique constraint that ensures this behaviour.

includedPullRequests.title: Fix Spurious Pump Alerts
includedPullRequests.body:
## Summary

The alerting rework routes pump-thrown alerts through the LoopKit protocol as intended. Tapping a notification calls acknowledgeAlert on the PM, no auto-ack anywhere. But it missed one spot: dismissing the in-app banner (and swiping away the UN) routed to snooze only, so the ack was never sent to the PM. With the app foregrounded, a pod alert could stay unacknowledged on the pod indefinitely unless the user also tapped the notification, leaving pods beeping on their repeat schedule (15 min expiration reminder, hourly low reservoir/expired) and alerts stuck as unacknowledged.

Dismissal is the user's response, so requestSnooze now also forwards the ack to the issuing device manager via acknowledgeOnDevice(), extracted from handleAcknowledgement. The forward is guarded on a registered AlertResponder – only device managers register (currently just the pump manager) – so Trio-internal alerts (glucose for Libre 3 and other CGms that require it, not-looping, algorithm) are unaffected, and PMs with no-op ack's (Medtrum, Dana, Minimed) see no behavior change.

User-facing behavior is unchanged: swipe/tap away still auto-snoozes for 15 min and dismisses both presentations, **but** pump alerts are now properly acknowledged at the PM layer (e.g. AcknowledgeAlerts reaches the pod and silences its beeps).

Fixes #1135.

includedPullRequests.title: Remove core data fetch limits (already time predicate limited)
includedPullRequests.body:
We had a few fetch limits in place that were potentially leading to fetch results, especially for pump history.

E.g. `getPumpHistory()` uses `pumpHistoryLast24h` but `fetchLimit: 288`.  With TBRs every 5-15 min (96-288/day) plus SMBs and manual user-initiated boluses, there are well over 288 events per day. This could potentially lead to the most‑recent 288 fetch result truncating pump eventing, covering less than 24h.

Similar for fetching glucose, where the predicate did not exclude potential manual glucose entries, leading to a false assumption of one reading per 5 minutes (288(day).

highlights.title: Sensor lifetime indicator on glucose bobble
changes: A colored arc around the glucose bobble drains clockwise to show the sensor's remaining life.
A short tag below the bobble shows time left or a status word like "warming up" or "sensor expired".
Warmup and sensor-failed states hide the arc and show the glucose as a muted "– –".
G7 sensors now count down through the grace period so the bobble's time tag matches the arc.
The glucose simulator adds a Sensor Lifecycle picker so you can preview these sensor states without hardware.

sources.title: nightscout/Trio#1205: Add CGMStatusHighlight Handling: CGM sensor lifetime indicator (arc + tag) – Prepare Libre 3/3+ Integration [2/3]

includedPullRequests.title: Add CGMStatusHighlight Handling: CGM sensor lifetime indicator (arc + tag) – Prepare Libre 3/3+ Integration [2/3]
includedPullRequests.body: ## Summary Adds a sensor lifetime indicator to the home-screen glucose bobble: - **Outer arc** — drains clockwise from 12 o'clock over the sensor's life, sourced from `cgmLifecycleProgress`. Green → orange → red based on `progressState`. - **Tag below the bobble** — short label showing time remaining (`5d 14h`, `22h left`) or a status word (`warming up`, `calibrate`, `sensor expired`, `sensor error`). - Six sensor states: normal / expiring soon / warmup / calibration required / expired / sensor failed. Warmup + failure hide the arc and mute the glucose number to `– –`. - **G7 grace period** — bobble countdown falls back to `sensorEndsAt` once `sensorExpiresAt` passes (was collapsing to `<1m` while the arc still showed ~12h). ‼️ **CAVEAT** This does for users using CGMBLEKit (Dexcom G5/G6/One) through a fork and an addition to CGMBLEKit, not via the current version of the submodule as found in `dev` and `main`. LibreTransmitter has not been tested, should work though. xDrip4iOS will get an update providing us with CGM lifecycle and status highlight _like_ data, but that has not been shipped or checked in to xDrip4iOS yet (thanks to @paulplant for the collab on this) Some Follow-up PRs like needed. --- ## How sensor lifetime is resolved `HomeStateModel.sensorExpiresAt(for:lifecycle:)` resolves the expiration date in this order: 1. **Direct read** when the manager exposes it (e.g., `sensorExpiresAt`). For G7, falls back to `sensorEndsAt` once the primary lifetime has passed so the grace window is counted down instead of clamped to zero. 2. **Reverse-derive** from `activatedAt + percentComplete`: `totalLifetime = elapsed / percentComplete`, `expiresAt = activatedAt + totalLifetime`. Picks up `activatedAt` via the same type-check pattern already used in `PluginSource.readCGMResult` 3. **Fallback** — `XX%` label when neither is available. ## Extended CGM Simulator: Scenario picker The glucose simulator exposes `cgmStatusHighlight` + `cgmLifecycleProgress` so the indicator is exercisable without real CGM hardware. CGM Settings → Glucose Simulator → "Sensor Lifecycle Scenario" picker flips between all six states. To match the data shape a real CGM would deliver in each state: - Non-active scenarios (warmup / calibration / expired / failed) **stop emitting fresh readings**, so the bobble's stale path fires and the highlight's `imageName` surfaces. - Picker change also **drops `GlucoseStored` rows inside the 12 min freshness window**, so the bobble flips immediately instead of waiting 12 minutes for organic aging. - `HomeStateModel`'s 30 s refresh timer reads `cgmLifecycleProgress` / `cgmStatusHighlight` from the simulator source when no live `CGMManager` is set, so the same code path serves both real and simulated sensors. ## Screenshots | Nominal | Expiring soon | Expired | |--------|--------|--------| | | | | | Warmup | Calibration | Error | |--------|--------|--------| | > | | | Addresses #304 , picks up where #981 left off (thanks for the previous work @bastiaanv ).

highlights.title: More reliable bolus progress reporting
changes: Bolus progress updates are now handled on a single internal queue to avoid race conditions.
Creating and clearing the bolus progress reporter are serialized so rapid start/stop transitions don't overwrite each -.

Trio clears the stale token and attempts to replay recent database changes.
If replay fails, Trio refreshes all stored data and switches to the current token so updates continue.

includedPullRequests.title: APSManager thread safety fixes (Part 3)
includedPullRequests.body:
Closes the remaining mutable-state race in APSManager around bolus
progress reporting. Final piece split out of the original https://github.com/nightscout/Trio/pull/1145 to have its own architectural discussion.

**BolusProgressState**

  The old `bolusReporter` property was a plain mutable `DoseProgressReporter?`
  mutated from a Combine sink on processQueue (bolusTrigger transitions)
  and concurrently from `cancelBolus` (async context). No isolation.

  Replaced with a private `BolusProgressState` actor that owns the
  reporter and serializes add/remove-observer calls.

**Reorder race in the bolusTrigger sink**

  Pre-change, the sink called `createBolusReporter()` or `clearBolusReporter()`
  directly, both of which were sync. Making them async opens a window
  where two unstructured Tasks (one per emission) could reorder against
  each other at the actor — unstructured Tasks enter an actor in
  scheduler order, not the order they were spawned. A fast bolus
  end-then-start transition (real in practice) could land setReporter
  before its matching clear and wipe the fresh reporter.

  Both transitions are now funneled through a single Task per sink
  emission, awaiting in-order at the actor.

**doseProgressReporterDidUpdate**

  The `DoseProgressObserver` protocol method is sync, so the call to
  `clearBolusReporter()` (now async) is wrapped in a Task. The race that
  would normally make this dangerous is exactly what the generation
  token in BolusProgressState protects against.

includedPullRequests.title: Refresh stale persistent history token for CoreData
includedPullRequests.body:
When Trio calls `fetchPersistentHistory` it logs errors. One type of
error, the `NSPersistentHistoryTokenExpiredError` error will cause
Trio to stop refreshing data. This error can happen for a few reasons,
like not using Trio of a while or updating the database.

The fix is to refresh all objects manually and reset the persistent
history token, i.e., `lastHistoryToken` stored in UserDefaults.

This error is extremely hard to test, but I had a device stuck in this
state and confirmed the fix.

includedPullRequests.title: Make sure that autosens has a profile to work with
includedPullRequests.body:
The Swift version of autosens expects to have a profile object, so if
you start with oref-swift from the start the current version will
throw an exception on autosens since there is no profile, and the
algorithm will never make progress.

This change swaps the order of autosens and profile creation. Profile
creation doesn't depend on autosens, so this change is safe and
enables systems set up as oref-swift from the start run.

Note: There was a tiny amount of parallelism in the previous version,
but not enough to justify so I removed it to make the code more
straightforward.

Thanks to @mountrcg for reporting this originally.

highlights.title: Prevent duplicate loops and tidy background tasks
changes: Trio will no longer start two loop cycles at the same time.
Loops now enforce the minimum interval between runs to avoid running too soon.
Background task handling was simplified so each loop uses a local background task instead of a shared one.
Loop completion and statistics are recorded at a single exit point so outcomes are reported consistently.

sources.title: APSManager thread safety fixes Part 2: LoopGuard actor + background-task lifecycle

includedPullRequests.title: APSManager thread safety fixes (Part 2)
includedPullRequests.body:
Closes the loop-serialization race and reworks the background-task
lifecycle around the loop's async scope. Sequel to Part 1, split out
of the original https://github.com/nightscout/Trio/pull/1145 to discuss LoopGuard + background-task handling separately from the bolus-progress actor.

**LoopGuard**

  The old check-then-act pattern read `isLooping.value`, returned from
  `canStartNewLoop()`, then later (after a suspension point) called
  `isLooping.send(true)`. Two concurrent `recommendsLoop` events could
  both observe `false` before either had sent `true`, so loop() could
  run twice. Replaced by a private `LoopGuard` actor that performs the
  check-and-mark-running step as a single atomic operation. isLooping
  becomes a downstream notification mechanism.

**Background-task lifecycle**

Removed the Task in the expiration handler which didn't provide a guarantee to be executed. Aligned with the example in https://developer.apple.com/documentation/uikit/extending-your-app-s-background-execution-time. Side note: we probably need to refactor this when moving to Swift 6 as the expiration handler closure is Sendable and captures and mutates the var backgroundTaskID which isn't allowed when strict concurrency checking is on.

**Loop lifecycle consolidation**

  `canStartNewLoop`, `setupLoop`, `loopCompleted` are folded into
  loop() and a new single-exit-point `finalizeLoop`. `executeLoop` no
  longer calls finalize itself; the caller owns lifecycle. Open and
  closed loop paths in executeLoop now share the stats-recording
  block, with `enactDetermination` gated by `settings.closedLoop` at
  the end.

  `calculateLoopInterval` now takes `loopStartDate` as a parameter
  instead of reading the instance `lastLoopStartDate` — keeps the
  helper independent of the surrounding mutation order in loop().

highlights.title: APSManager thread-safety and UI update fixes
changes: Reduced chance of crashes by removing unsafe force unwraps and using weaker references in closures.
Pump and basal state updates now run on the main thread to avoid UI race conditions and glitches.
Manual temporary basal changes now reliably trigger a loop run when they finish or are cancelled.
Time‑in‑Range statistics are computed directly to avoid redundant background context calls.

includedPullRequests.title: APSManager thread safety fixes (Part 1)
includedPullRequests.body:
This will be the first of at least 3 Pull requests targeted at optimizing the thread safety of the APSManager. As suggested by @kingst in PR #1145 I splitted my initial PR in several smaller PRs to be able to better review the changes made.

Changes made in this PR:

- remove unused `viewContext`
- move `tir()` out of a nested perform closure
- add `[weak self]` to combine sinks and `privateContext` perform closures
- move combine operations to receive on the main thread to avoid race conditions
- remove force unwraps

includedPullRequests.title: Remove unused convertGlucose function
includedPullRequests.body:
This PR removes an unused function from the core determine basal logic in oref-swift: `convertGlucose`. In the original JS implementation, several output properties and reason string values are run through a `convert_bg` function that converts the glucose value to `mmol` based on the `profile.outUnits` value.

However, `profile.outUnits` is always in `.mgdl`, and should always be in `.mgdl` so we should remove this function. The path goes:
- The convert function branches based on the value of `profile.outUnits`
- `profile.outUnits` is set from `bgTarget.userPreferredUnits`
- all of the write paths for the file that saves the `bgTarget` value store these values as mg/dl and hard code `userPreferredUnits` to `.mgdl`.

Unit tests still pass with this change.

highlights.title: Opt-in Swift oref0 algorithm
changes: Adds an opt-in native Swift port of Trio's oref0 dosing algorithm (IOB, Meal, Autosens, DetermineBasal).
Adds a Settings toggle at Algorithm → Additionals → Use Swift Oref to enable the feature.
When enabled, Trio uses the Swift implementation for dosing instead of the bundled JavaScript runtime.
A green 'Swift Oref' badge is shown in the Loop Status view while the Swift path is active.
The JavaScript path remains the default and is unchanged unless you opt in.

sources.title: nightscout/Trio#1141: Port oref0 Algorithm from Javascript to Swift

highlights.title: Clamp very-low CGM readings to 39 mg/dL
changes: CGM readings below 39 mg/dL are raised to 39 before storage or use.
Backfilled CGM data is clamped the same way.
The original raw low reading and timestamp are kept in debug logs for diagnostics.
User-entered fingerstick (manual) glucose values are not altered.

sources.title: nightscout/Trio#1159: Fix: Floor CGM glucose at 39 mg/dL in GlucoseStorage

highlights.title: Glucose fetch follows meal absorption time
changes: Glucose readings used for meal/COB now cover your Max Meal Absorption Time plus 30 minutes.
The previous hard 6-hour cap on glucose history is removed so longer MMAT settings are respected.
Meal/COB should no longer spike above entered carbs or cliff-drop when MMAT exceeds 6 hours.
This behavior is applied consistently for both JS-based and native oref implementations.

sources.title: nightscout/Trio#1171: Change fetchLimit for glucose oref input to be dynamic based on maxMealAbsorptionTime #920

highlights.title: dynISF disabled only for high temporary targets
changes: Dynamic sensitivity adjustments (dynISF) will now activate for users whose profile target is above 118.
dynISF is only turned off when a temporary target is set at or above the half-basal exercise target (default 160).

sources.title: nightscout/Trio#1189: Change profile target dynISF condition with HighTTRaisesSensitivity

highlights.title: Omnipod driver and Live Activity fixes
changes: Legacy OmniKit and OmniBLE drivers were removed; OmnipodKit is now the only Omnipod option.
Live Activity now shows active temp targets as a green band and fixes indefinite overrides and widget labels.
Watch app recovers from long staleness and refreshes quickly instead of getting stuck.
Today's Meals stats now show carbs, fat, and protein correctly instead of zeros.

includedPullRequests.title: Port oref0 Algorithm from Javascript to Swift
includedPullRequests.body:
## Summary

Native Swift port of Trio's [JS-based oref0 algorithm](https://github.com/nightscout/trio-oref), replacing the JavaScript bridge with a first-class Swift implementation across all core algorithm modules: IOB, Meal, Autosens, and DetermineBasal (including Dynamic ISF).

### Background
Trio has historically executed its dosing algorithm by calling into a bundled JavaScript runtime (`trio-oref`). This port replaces that execution path end-to-end in Swift, removing the JS bridge dependency for users who — currently! — opt in. The port was developed between February 2025 and April 2026.

More details to be added at later point. The research paper produced in connection with this port will be provided at a later date.

###  Verification
Parity between the JavaScript and Swift implementations was verified through:

* **Mechanistic in silico simulation**: structural comparison of algorithm logic
* **Shadow execution replay**: 736,480 real-world algorithm invocations were replayed through both implementations and their outputs compared, covering all Swift modules

Notable behaviours verified include: IoB with long pump suspends, zombie carb detection, `currentDeviation` null handling in meal, `avgDelta` and `getGlucoseStatus` rounding, Dynamic ISF `tddRatio` / `insulinFactor` / `sensitivityRatio` outputs, and `expectedDelta` edge cases.

### Enabling

This is **opt-in**. The JavaScript path remains the default and is unchanged.

**Settings → Algorithm → Additionals → Use Swift Oref**

- **Off** (default): algorithm runs entirely in JavaScript, JS outputs drive insulin dosing
- **On**: algorithm runs entirely in Swift, Swift outputs drive insulin dosing

When enabled, a green `Swift Oref` badge appears in the Loop Status view to make it clear which path is active.

### Publication relating to this port 
Screven, T., Zhu, Z., Cengiz, D., Lal, R. A., Hood, K. K., & King, S. T. (2026). [*A Principled Framework for Safe Algorithm Updates in Automated Insulin Delivery Systems*](https://arxiv.org/html/2606.13882v1). arXiv:2606.13882v1.

includedPullRequests.title: Fix: Floor CGM glucose at 39 mg/dL in GlucoseStorage
includedPullRequests.body:
## Summary

Addresses nightscout/Trio-dev#631 — since LibreTransmitter ignores Abbott given limits and propagates raw glucose, oref can receive values below the official 39 mg/dL CGM floor (LibreTransmitter explicitly bypasses the Libre algorithm's floor, forwarding values down to 1 mg/dL). On dev those sub-39 readings are silently dropped by the JS oref glucose-get-last filter (>38), leaving determination with no fresh BG and the pump stuck in whatever temp it was last on. The reporter saw ~1h 7min of no Swift determinations with a BG nadir of 23 mg/dL.

This PR adds a defensive chokepoint in BaseGlucoseStorage:

  - New Config.minimumGlucose = 39 and a private clampToMinimum(_:) helper that clamps both glucose and sgv on each
  BloodGlucose, emitting debug(.deviceManager, ...) with the raw value and timestamp so the original reading is preserved
  in support logs.
  - storeGlucose and backfillGlucose both run their input through clampToMinimum before any filtering or persistence.
  storeCGMState receives the clamped array too.
  - addManualGlucose is intentionally left untouched — user-entered fingerstick values are not silently rewritten.

  The floor aligns with BloodGlucose.isStateValid (already sgv >= 39) and FetchGlucoseManager.minimumSmoothedGlucose: 39.

includedPullRequests.title: Change fetchLimit for glucose oref input to be dynamic based on maxMealAbsorptionTime #920
includedPullRequests.body:
## Summary
The glucose history fed into the meal/COB algorithm was capped at a fixed fetchLimit: 72 (72 readings × 5 min = 6h).
That constant predates the user-selectable maxMealAbsorptionTime (MMAT) setting, which ranges 4–10h. When MMAT exceeds 6h, the COB calculation considers carb absorption out to MMAT hours but only has 6h of glucose to work with.

The result is the bug reported in #920: mealCOB rises after a carb entry (to an amount larger than the carbs entered)
as larger absorption buckets slide out the back of the 6h window and are replaced by smaller front buckets, then crashes to zero when the meal is evicted at mealTime + MMAT. This drives over-bolusing and hypo risk.

This affects both the JS oref0 path and the native oref-swift port, since the limit is applied upstream in OpenAPS.determineBasal before either path runs.

The fix makes the fetch limit dynamic: ceil(maxMealAbsorptionTime × 12) readings, so the glucose window always covers the full absorption window. Preferences are loaded up front (the existing async load is just hoisted and reused) to derive the limit before the parallel fetches. Tying it to the setting rather than a static cap means it can't silently drift again if the MMAT range changes.

Credit to @robertjwellwood for the diagnosis and simulation in #920.

## To Test

  - Build and run with default MMAT (6h), then verify COB behaves as before
  - Set MMAT to 8–10h, enter a carb dose, and confirm COB decays smoothly toward 0 without rising above the entered amount or cliff-dropping
  - Verify both useSwiftOref on and off produce consistent COB trajectories (so JS-based oref and native Swift port)
  - Confirm glucoseFetchLimit scales as expected (4h→48, 6h→72, 10h→120)

~~- Before this change, condition checked whether highTTRaisesSens was active AND user's profile target >= 118~~
~~- We received an issue report by a parent whose child runs with profile target set at >118, thus dynISF NEVER activated~~
~~- Change now moves this check condition to a saner target of halfBasalExerciseTarget, to ONLY disable dynISF if the user ACTUALLY sets a TT at a target value >= HBT (which by default is 160)~~

Removes a legacy (pre Trio) dynISF condition that would disable dynISF.

includedPullRequests.title: Trio v0.8.2 patch release
includedPullRequests.body:
## What's Changed At A Glance

- **OmnipodKit is now the sole Omnipod driver** — OmniBLE and OmniKit submodules removed; "All Omnipod Types" is the only Omnipod entry in Add Pump and Onboarding
- **Live Activity Enhancements / Fixes:**
   -  Temp target on chart — active temp targets now render as a green band on the Live Activity chart, with a named badge alongside the override badge
   - Ondefinite override fix — overrides with no set duration no longer collapse the chart's rule mark to the current moment
   - COB label and widget string fixes — corrected the COB string and localized all widget item labels
- **Watch connectivity fix** — massively improve watch performance; stale data on watch now updates in just a moment vs. getting stuck during processing.
- **Fix Stats Meals showing zero macros for current day** — today's carbs, fat, and protein now always appear correctly in the Meals chart
---

## Full List of PRs in `0.8.2`

| PR | Title | Author |
|---|---|---|
| [#1180](https://github.com/nightscout/Trio/pull/1180) | Add temp target display to Live Activity lock screen widget | [@t1dude](https://github.com/t1dude) |
| [#1182](https://github.com/nightscout/Trio/pull/1182) | Update CODEOWNERS: add @marionbarker and @kingst | [@dnzxy](https://github.com/dnzxy) |
| [#1184](https://github.com/nightscout/Trio/pull/1184) | Fix COB string, localize Live Activity widget item labels | [@dnzxy](https://github.com/dnzxy) |
| [#1188](https://github.com/nightscout/Trio/pull/1188) | Fix Stats Meals showing zero macros for current day | [@dnzxy](https://github.com/dnzxy) |
| [#1190](https://github.com/nightscout/Trio/pull/1190) | Fix watch app back-sync after long staleness | [@dnzxy](https://github.com/dnzxy) |
| [#1192](https://github.com/nightscout/Trio/pull/1192) | Fix indefinite override not drawn in Live Activity chart | [@t1dude](https://github.com/t1dude) |
| [#1200](https://github.com/nightscout/Trio/pull/1200) | Remove Legacy Omnipod Submodules (OmniKit, OmniBLE) | [@dnzxy](https://github.com/dnzxy) |

---

## Contributors

@dnzxy @t1dude @marv-out

highlights.title: Consolidate Omnipod options into OmnipodKit
changes: Pump picker now shows a single "All Omnipod Types" option instead of separate Eros and DASH entries.
Onboarding and pump setup use one Omnipod choice instead of separate Eros/DASH options.
Omnipod onboarding basal-rate picker allows 0 U/hr (DASH minimum), which may not enforce Eros's 0.05 U/hr minimum.
Privacy and telemetry texts now reference OmnipodKit instead of the removed OmniKit/OmniBLE.

sources.title: nightscout/Trio#1200: Remove Legacy Omnipod Submodules (OmniKit, OmniBLE)

includedPullRequests.title: Remove Legacy Omnipod Submodules (OmniKit, OmniBLE)
includedPullRequests.body:
## Summary
Removes the legacy `OmniBLE` (DASH) and `OmniKit` (Eros) submodules and all their platform-specific code paths, consolidating both Omnipod variants under the new `OmnipodKit` submodule (`OmniPumpManager`), added and shipped with Trio `v0.8.1`.

**Changes:**
- Deletes `OmniBLE` and `OmniKit` git submodules and their `.gitmodules` entries
- Bumps `OmnipodKit` to `d68699c`
- Removes `OmniBLEPumpManager` and `OmnipodPumpManager` from `staticPumpManagers`, `DeviceDataManager`, `PumpSetupView`, and all pump-type enums — replaced by `OmniPumpManager`
- Collapses the separate "Omnipod Eros" / "Omnipod DASH" pump-selection buttons into a single "All Omnipod Types" entry in `HomeRootView` and `PumpConfigRootView`
- Merges `omnipodEros` / `omnipodDash` onboarding enum cases into a single `.omni` case; adds a `FIXME` noting that Eros min-basal-rate differentiation is not yet handled
- Removes OmniBLE/OmniKit build targets, framework references, and test schemes from `project.pbxproj` and `Trio.xcscheme`
- Drops OmniBLE/OmniKit Crowdin localisation sources and swiftformat exclusions
- Updates telemetry comment and `PRIVACY_POLICY.md` to reference OmnipodKit instead of OmniBLE

**Note:** The `.omni` onboarding basal-rate picker currently uses the DASH minimum (0 U/hr). Eros requires a minimum of 0.05 U/hr but the two variants can no longer be distinguished at that step — tracked in the inline `FIXME`.

includedPullRequests.title: Fix Stats Meals showing zero macros for current day
includedPullRequests.body:
## Summary 
* `calculateAveragesForDateRange` filtered cached daily totals with a strict `date >= startDate` against day-keys that are startOfDay. The .day-interval
* initial scroll position is startOfDay(today) + 1s, which leaves today's `today 00:00:00` bucket just outside the range, zeroing today's carbs/fat/protein in the Meals chart (reported as #1181).
* Scrolling slightly back into yesterday made today appear again.

## Fix
* Compare against the day window (overlap semantics) instead of the day's start instant: a cached day belongs in the range if any moment of it overlaps.
* Backward-compatible for `.week`/`.month`/`.total` because those intervals already align `startDate` to midnight.

## Additional Change
* Simplify calculateAndCacheDailyAverages (no behavior change) 
* `dailyMealStats` is already one entry per day from `fetchMealStats`, so the previous `Dictionary(grouping:)` and per-group reduce + divide-by-count was a passthrough (count==1, total/1==total).
* Replace with a direct `Dictionary(_, uniquingKeysWith:)` re-key by `startOfDay`, keeping the defensive sum-merge in case a future caller constructs MealStats with mid-day timestamps.

---

Addresses #1181.

changes: The watch ignores incoming state messages older than 15 minutes to avoid replaying a backlog.
Duplicate or older watch-state updates are skipped so the watch avoids repeated UI updates.

sources.title: nightscout/Trio#1190: Fix watch app back-sync after long staleness

Fix watch app back-sync after long staleness * Unify didReceiveMessage + didReceiveUserInfo through one handleIncomingWatchStatePayload * Add guard on wall-clock staleness, then perform monotonicity dedupe * Add state.date with Date() at send time, not build time * Drop lastSent < state.date guard * Watch-requested re-pushes no longer get silenced when no CGM tick has bumped the build-time date Potentially addresses #1162

includedPullRequests.title: Fix spurious 'Bolus failed' notification after manual bolus
includedPullRequests.body:
fixes #1150 
The post-bolus determineBasalSync() call was inside the bolus do/catch, so any algorithm error (e.g. 'Not enough glucose data' during CGM warm-up) was misreported as a bolus failure even though the pump had already delivered the bolus successfully. Wrap the algorithm refresh in its own do/catch so it cannot trigger bolusDidFail or a pumpError notification.

includedPullRequests.title: Trio v0.8.1 patch release
includedPullRequests.body:
## Summary
Rolls three recently-landed tracks into a patch release: the new OmnipodKit pump manager and a security-driven dependency bump.

## What's Included

  1. OmnipodKit pump manager (Eros + DASH unified) — adds loopandlearn/OmnipodKit as a new submodule plus the wiring to surface it in the Add Pump flow as "All Omnipod Types". Pod type (Eros vs. DASH) is selected at activation rather than at the pump-manager level. OmniKit and OmniBLE remain in place unchanged — existing users on those drivers see no behavior change; the new driver is purely opt-in until a user explicitly selects it on next pod start.

  2. Security: fastlane 2.235.0 / jwt 3.2.0 — bumps fastlane 2.233.1 → 2.235.0, which relaxes its transitive jwt < 3 cap and lets the resolver land jwt 3.2.0+. Closes Dependabot alert #14 (ruby-jwt empty-key HMAC bypass, sibling of CVE-2026-44351).
  CI/release tooling only, no shipped-app code surface change. 

  3. Fix Telemetry Daily Sends — continuous background pings were not being correctly throttled to once per day. The daily send logic is now enforced properly. 

It also includes small fixes added recently to dev:
* Fix Spurious "Bolus Failed" Notification After Manual Bolus (https://github.com/nightscout/Trio/pull/1151)
* Telemetry Payload Improvements (#1172)

includedPullRequests.title: Update telemetry payload: fix buildDate format, add locale/TZ/appName
includedPullRequests.body:
Fixes a few issues identified with the Trio telemetry payloads:

* On non-English locales, the buildDate was improperly formatted. e.g., a builder on a French language-default OS would emit `lun. mai 2`. Fix: force capture-build-details to POSIX default locale (english).
* Parse the timestamp and emit as YYYY-MM-DD instead of “Tue May 26” format
* Add iOS system locale + timezone to telemetry, with the above bug as motivation for why we should track this. Plus, this will be beneficial for users when managing translations.
* Add appName to telemetry -- hardcoded as "Trio", but can be overridden for forks

includedPullRequests.title: Release/v0.8.0
includedPullRequests.body:
**For detailed release notes, see: https://github.com/nightscout/Trio/releases/tag/v0.8.0**

## What's Changed At A Glance

* **Anonymous opt-out Telemetry**: helps the team understand how Trio is used, backed by Apple App Attest
* **Therapy settings upload to Tidepool**: Trio now pushes basal, CR, ISF, and target settings into Tidepool alongside treatment data
* **Delete glucose from Nightscout**: glucose entries deleted in Trio now propagate to Nightscout
* **Override confirmation dialogs**: optional confirmation step before activating an override or temporary target
* **Bolus progress in Treatments view**: see the active bolus and cancel it without leaving the Treatments screen
* **Context menus** on all swipeable history list items (long-press alternative to swipe)
* **Settings Search scroll-to and highlight**: tapping a search result now scrolls to and briefly highlights the matched row
* **Bolus safety checks unified**: Shortcut-driven boluses now get the same max-bolus, max-IOB, and recent-bolus validation as APNS remote boluses
* **Nightscout site change events corrected**: fixed false positives for Medtronic, added a proper `SiteChange` event for Trio History
* **Glucose smoothing window fix**: smoothing now correctly uses the *newest* 350 readings, not the oldest
* **Submodule updates**: OmniBLE (pod keep-alive now part of `main`), DanaKit, MedtrumKit, CGMBLEKit

includedPullRequests.title: Add scroll-to and highlight for settings search results
includedPullRequests.body:
## Summary

Adds scroll-to and highlight behavior when tapping a settings search result. Previously, search navigated to the correct screen but left the user to find the setting themselves. Now it scrolls directly to the setting and briefly highlights it.

This is the first PR of what will I'm sure be several. This contains the core logic and enables it for Algorithm > Dynamic Settings. Remaining screens will follow in subsequent PRs (many will add a single `.settingsHighlightScroll()` line per view).

## How it works

Two view modifiers drive the feature:

- **`.settingsHighlightScroll()`** Added once per destination view. Wraps the view in a `ScrollViewReader` and watches for a highlighted setting, then scrolls to it.
- **`.settingsSearchTarget(label:)`** Added to each setting row. Sets `.id(label)` so `ScrollViewReader` can find it, and adds a highlight animation that flashes when the label matches.

`SettingInputSection` (the shared component used by most settings) applies `.settingsSearchTarget(label:)` automatically using its existing `label` parameter. Most screens will only need a single `.settingsHighlightScroll()` line to opt in. Custom sections (like the Dynamic ISF picker) will need a `.settingsSearchTarget(label:)` added manually.

The search result list now uses `NavigationLink(value: SearchResultTarget(...))` which carries both the destination screen and the scroll target label. On navigation, the label is passed to `SettingsSearchHighlight` (an `@Observable` object in the environment), which triggers the scroll and highlight.

`scrollTargetLabels` on `SettingItem` handles cases where the search term differs from the UI label (e.g. searching "Max IOB" scrolls to "Maximum Insulin on Board (IOB)"). This mapping is data-only and has no effect until a screen opts-in with `.settingsHighlightScroll()`.

## Demo

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/e8743199-cb06-431f-afb3-48f92045a9de

## Scroll demo on smaller screen.

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/51a5d7b0-7dc7-4a84-b5fd-45dbc6183215

includedPullRequests.title: Settings Search with Scroll-to and Highlight for the Matched Result
includedPullRequests.body:
## Summary
Settings search used to surface the right sub-screen but drop you at the top, leaving users to scan for the setting that
actually matched. This PR makes search results jump directly to the matched control and highlight it briefly.

##  Changes
  - New `SettingsSearchHighlight.swift` view modifier (`.settingsHighlightScroll(…)`) that pairs a settings row with the
  search target, scrolls to it on tap-through, and animates a transient highlight.
  - Modifier applied to 16 settings sub-screens so every searchable surface participates.
  - `scrollTargetLabels` mappings on each `SettingItem` for cases where the search hit isn't itself a visible row, e.g.,
  "Carbs Required Threshold" → "Show Carbs Required Badge" (the threshold field only renders when the badge is enabled),
  "High Glucose Target" → "Enable SMB With High Glucose", Bolus Calculator conditional fields, etc. Keeps the user-typed
  term as the entry point while routing to the visible parent.
  - Introduced `SettingsSearchTests.swift` unit coverage for the mapping resolution

Addresses https://github.com/nightscout/Trio/issues/1095
Authored by @gordolio.

includedPullRequests.title: Add scroll targets and fix search index labels for settings search
includedPullRequests.body:
Part 3 of #1095 

- Replace .listRowBackground(Color.chart) with .settingsSearchTarget(label:) on 7 User Interface sections so they become scroll targets for search
- Add .settingsHighlightScroll() to User Interface screen
- Add scrollTargetLabels mappings for Units/Limits, SMB, Target Behavior, Additionals, and User interface
- Fix incorrect searchContents in Bolus Calculator and User Interface

## About `scrollTargetLabels`
`scrollTargetLabels` maps the search term to the actual UI label:
```swift
scrollTargetLabels: [
    "Max IOB": "Maximum Insulin on Board (IOB)",
    "Max Bolus": "Maximum Bolus",
]
```

There are two cases where these are needed:
1. Abbreviated search terms - the search result/index uses a shorter/common name but the label within the UI shows the full name (e.g. "Max Basal" → "Maximum Basal Rate")
2. Multiple search terms targeting one section - several terms scroll to the same UI element
  - "Dark Mode", "Light Mode", and "Appearance" all scroll to the Appearance picker
  - "Cone" and "Lines" both scroll to the "Forecast Display Type"

includedPullRequests.title: Shared BolusSafetyValidator for APNS and Shortcut boluses
includedPullRequests.body:
## Summary

Extracts a shared `BolusSafetyValidator` service that centralises the safety checks previously inlined in the APNS remote-bolus path, and applies them to Shortcut-driven boluses as well.

**Checks applied:** max bolus, max IOB, recent-bolus 20% threshold.

## Remote commands (APNS)

Behaviour is unchanged. `TrioRemoteControl+Bolus` now calls the validator with `lookbackStart: Date(timeIntervalSince1970: payload.timestamp)`, so the 20% recent-bolus check still covers the interval between the command being sent and now, and existing rejection messages are preserved. (Minor side-effect: an unavailable IOB now rejects with a logged message instead of throwing.)

## Shortcuts

Shortcut boluses previously checked only the "max bolus" limit. They now get the same full set of checks. Because the Shortcut has no caller-provided origin timestamp, the recent-bolus check uses a 6-minute rolling window (`BolusSafetyEvaluator.recentBolusWindowMinutes`).

Closes #531

includedPullRequests.title: Fix Apple Health flag by checking for sample type permission
includedPullRequests.body:
A tester reported appleHealthEnabled: false despite a working Apple Health integration.

Cause: `hasGrantedFullWritePermissions` required all five Trio-managed sample types (glucose, insulin, carbs, fat, protein) to be authorized.

Changes in `TelemetryClient.buildPayload`: 
- `appleHealthEnabled` → true when any write permission is granted (matches the state-based nightscoutPaired /
  tidepoolPaired pattern already in place).
- New `appleHealthWrites: { glucose, insulin, carbs, fat, protein }`check per-type authorization snapshot. Reuses the existing `checkWriteToHealthPermissions` of HealthKitManager.

includedPullRequests.title: Add Anonymous Telemetry
includedPullRequests.body: ## Overview * Adds an opt-out anonymous usage check-in modeled on LoopFollow#626 * Diagnostics consent now has three states: Full Sharing (crashes + telemetry), Crash Reports Only, and Disabled. Default is Full Sharing (opt-out) * Existing Crashlytics wiring is unchanged * Add new flag * Onboarding and Settings → App Diagnostics show the new selection option * Existing users get a one-time migration sheet on first foreground. * New TelemetryClient lives under Services/Telemetry - `TelemetryAttestor` owns `DCAppAttestService` key generation (Secure Enclave-backed), Keychain-persisted registration, and per-ping assertion building. - `TelemetryClient.send()` skips on `DCAppAttestService.isSupported == false` (simulator), short-circuits on a sticky "forbidden" flag set by a 403 from `/api/attest/register`, and posts to `/checkin` with the three `X-AppAttest-*` headers. Assertion is built over `SHA256(payload || challenge)` per the server spec. - `app_id` (`TEAMID.bundle.id`) is read from the embedded `embedded.mobileprovision` file. The `SecTask*` APIs aren't Swift-exposed on iOS; this is the standard workaround. Trio always ships with an embedded mobileprovision (sideload / TestFlight), so this path is reliable. - Added the `com.apple.developer.devicecheck.appattest-environment = development` entitlement (matching the existing `aps-environment`). * Extended PRIVACY_POLICY.md with Telemetry section This PR is still draft until the server side is reviewed in tandem. ## Screenshots | Onboarding | Migration Popup | Settings | |--------|--------|--------| | | | | ## TODOs - [x] Update endpoint once telemetry backend is set up - [x] Test check-in pings and upload end-to-end

includedPullRequests.title: Fix help buttons on Dynamic Settings page
includedPullRequests.body:
The question mark buttons for Adjustment Factor, Weighted Average of TDD, and Adjust Basal on the Dynamic Settings page did nothing when tapped. Only the Dynamic Insulin Sensitivity one worked.

Regression from the cold-tap help-sheet fix. That commit replaced three `@State` vars with a single `HintPayload?` and a derived `shouldDisplayHintBinding`, but the binding's setter only handled `false`. `SettingInputSection` toggles `shouldDisplayHint` from false to true and then assigns the verbose hint based on the new value — since the toggle to `true` was dropped, the next line read `false` and assigned `nil`, so nothing was presented.

Fix lets the setter handle `true` by seeding a placeholder payload. The verbose-hint binding then immediately replaces it with the real label and content on the same action.
